narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an impressive LSAT score of 39, you've clearly put in the effort to build a strong academic foundation, and your middle family income bracket reflects a background many students share. You're planning to attend law school full-time, which is the typical path for most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 52.4%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates with the lowest predicted risk. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades that year placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers, which stood out strongly to the model. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed notably in the same direction, suggesting early academic challenges carried weight in the assessment. On a brighter note, your standout LSAT score worked significantly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other positive factor, while your above-average undergraduate GPA provided meaningful support too. Your family income and full-time enrollment status offered smaller boosts toward passing, aligning with typical successful profiles. Compared to average applicants—who rank around the 5th or 6th decile in both early and later years—your lower decile positions were a key departure, overshadowing your test score strengths despite your LSAT exceeding the dataset average. This combination tipped the scales, as the model weighs consistent school performance heavily for bar exam outcomes. While disappointing, this isn't a reflection of your potential as a whole—your LSAT shows real aptitude. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, gathering stronger letters addressing those early grades, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews. You're capable of turning this around; many do with targeted improvements. Keep pushing—you've got the raw talent.
